cot_prefix: Okay, so I need to figure out if an 8x8 chessboard with two diagonally opposite corners removed can be tiled perfectly with 31 dominoes. Each domino covers two adjacent squares. Let me think about this step by step. First, I remember that a standard chessboard has 64 squares. If we remove two corners that are diagonally opposite, that leaves us with 62 squares. Since each domino covers two squares, 31 dominoes would cover exactly 62 squares. So the total number of squares is even, which is a good start. But just having an even number isn't enough, right? There must be some other condition. I recall something about coloring the chessboard. Normally, a chessboard has alternating black and white squares. Each domino, when placed on the board, will always cover one black and one white square, regardless of its orientation. So, if the entire board has an equal number of black and white squares, then domino tiling is possible. But if there's an imbalance, then it might not be. Let me check that. Let's say the original chessboard has 32 black and 32 white squares. When we remove two diagonally opposite corners, what happens?
